title: How Neural Networks Extrapolate: From Feedforward to Graph Neural Networks

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## HOW NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE: FROM FEEDFORWARD TO GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS
**Keyulu Xu** _[†]_ **, Mozhi Zhang** _[‡]_ **, Jingling Li** _[‡]_ **, Simon S. Du** _[§]_ **, Ken-ichi Kawarabayashi** _[¶]_ **, Stefanie Jegelka** _[†]_

> _†_ Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)

> _‡_ University of Maryland

> _§_ University of Washington

> _¶_ National Institute of Informatics (NII)

keyulu@mit.edu, keyulux@csail.mit.edu

## ABSTRACT

We study how neural networks trained by gradient descent _extrapolate_ , i.e., what they learn outside the support of the training distribution. Previous works report mixed empirical results when extrapolating with neural networks: while feedforward neural networks, a.k.a. multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), do not extrapolate well in certain simple tasks, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) – structured networks with MLP modules – have shown some success in more complex tasks. Working towards a theoretical explanation, we identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate well. First, we quantify the observation that ReLU MLPs quickly converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin, which implies that ReLU MLPs do not extrapolate most nonlinear functions. But, they can provably learn a linear target function when the training distribution is sufficiently “diverse”. Second, in connection to analyzing the successes and limitations of GNNs, these results suggest a hypothesis for which we provide theoretical and empirical evidence: the success of GNNs in extrapolating algorithmic tasks to new data (e.g., larger graphs or edge weights) relies on encoding task-specific non-linearities in the architecture or features. Our theoretical analysis builds on a connection of over-parameterized networks to the neural tangent kernel. Empirically, our theory holds across different training settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Humans extrapolate well in many tasks. For example, we can apply arithmetics to arbitrarily large numbers. One may wonder whether a neural network can do the same and generalize to examples arbitrarily far from the training data (Lake et al., 2017). Curiously, previous works report mixed extrapolation results with neural networks. Early works demonstrate feedforward neural networks, a.k.a. multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), fail to extrapolate well when learning simple polynomial functions (Barnard & Wessels, 1992; Haley & Soloway, 1992). However, recent works show Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Scarselli et al., 2009), a class of structured networks with MLP building blocks, can generalize to graphs much larger than training graphs in challenging algorithmic tasks, such as predicting the time evolution of physical systems (Battaglia et al., 2016), learning graph algorithms (Velickovic et al., 2020), and solving mathematical equations (Lample & Charton, 2020).

To explain this puzzle, we formally study how neural networks trained by gradient descent (GD) extrapolate, i.e., what they learn outside the support of training distribution. We say a neural network extrapolates well if it learns a task outside the training distribution. At first glance, it may seem that neural networks can behave arbitrarily outside the training distribution since they have high capacity (Zhang et al., 2017) and are universal approximators (Cybenko, 1989; Funahashi, 1989; Hornik et al., 1989; Kurkova, 1992). However, neural networks are constrained by gradient descent training (Hardt et al., 2016; Soudry et al., 2018). In our analysis, we explicitly consider such implicit bias through the analogy of the training dynamics of over-parameterized neural networks and kernel regression via the _neural tangent kernel (NTK)_ (Jacot et al., 2018).

Figure 2: **How GNNs extrapolate.** Since MLPs can extrapolate well when learning linear functions, we hypothesize that GNNs can extrapolate well in dynamic programming (DP) tasks if we encode appropriate non-linearities in the architecture (left) and input representation (right; through domain knowledge or representation learning). The encoded non-linearities may not be necessary for interpolation, as they can be approximated by MLP modules, but they help extrapolation. We support the hypothesis theoretically (Theorem 3) and empirically (Figure 6).

Starting with feedforward networks, the simplest neural networks and building blocks of more complex architectures such as GNNs, we establish that the predictions of over-parameterized MLPs with ReLU activation trained by GD converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin. We prove a convergence rate for two-layer networks and empirically observe that convergence often occurs _close to_ the training data (Figure 1), which suggests ReLU MLPs cannot extrapolate well for most nonlinear tasks. We emphasize that our results do not follow from the fact that ReLU networks have finitely many linear regions (Arora et al., 2018; Hanin & Rolnick, 2019; Hein et al., 2019). While having finitely many linear regions implies ReLU MLPs _eventually_ become linear, it does not say whether MLPs will learn the correct target function close to the training distribution. In contrast, our results are non-asymptotic and quantify what kind of functions MLPs will learn close to the training distribution. Second, we identify a condition when MLPs extrapolate well: the task is linear and the geometry of the training distribution is sufficiently “diverse”. To our knowledge, our results are the first extrapolation results of this kind for feedforward neural networks.

We then relate our insights into feedforward neural networks to GNNs, to explain why GNNs extrapolate well in some algorithmic tasks. Prior works report successful extrapolation for tasks that can be solved by dynamic programming (DP) (Bellman, 1966), which has a computation structure aligned with GNNs (Xu et al., 2020). DP updates can often be decomposed into nonlinear and linear steps. Hence, we hypothesize that GNNs trained by GD can extrapolate well in a DP task, if we encode appropriate non-linearities in the _architecture_ and _input representation_ (Figure 2). Importantly, encoding non-linearities may be unnecessary for GNNs to _interpolate_ , because the MLP modules can easily learn many nonlinear functions inside the training distribution (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik et al., 1989; Xu et al., 2020), but it is crucial for GNNs to _extrapolate_ correctly. We prove this hypothesis for a simplified case using _Graph NTK_ (Du et al., 2019b). Empirically, we validate the hypothesis on three DP tasks: max degree, shortest paths, and _n_ -body problem. We show GNNs with appropriate architecture, input representation, and training distribution can predict well on graphs with unseen sizes, structures, edge weights, and node features. Our theory explains the empirical success in previous works and suggests their limitations: successful extrapolation relies on encoding task-specific non-linearities, which requires domain knowledge or extensive model search. From a broader standpoint, our insights go beyond GNNs and apply broadly to other neural networks.

To summarize, we study how neural networks extrapolate. First, ReLU MLPs trained by GD converge to linear functions along directions from the origin with a rate of _O_ (1 _/t_ ). Second, to explain why GNNs extrapolate well in some algorithmic tasks, we prove that ReLU MLPs can extrapolate well in linear tasks, leading to a hypothesis: a neural network can extrapolate well when appropriate nonlinearities are encoded into the architecture and features. We prove this hypothesis for a simplified case and provide empirical support for more general settings.

## 1.1 RELATED WORK

Early works show example tasks where MLPs do not extrapolate well, e.g. learning simple polynomials (Barnard & Wessels, 1992; Haley & Soloway, 1992). We instead show a _general_ pattern of how ReLU MLPs extrapolate and identify conditions for MLPs to extrapolate well. More recent works study the implicit biases induced on MLPs by gradient descent, for both the NTK and mean field regimes (Bietti & Mairal, 2019; Chizat & Bach, 2018; Song et al., 2018). Related to our results, some works show MLP predictions converge to “simple” piecewise linear functions, e.g., with few linear regions (Hanin & Rolnick, 2019; Maennel et al., 2018; Savarese et al., 2019; Williams et al., 2019). Our work differs in that none of these works explicitly studies extrapolation, and some focus only on one-dimensional inputs. Recent works also show that in high-dimensional settings of the NTK regime, MLP is asymptotically at most a linear predictor in certain scaling limits (Ba et al., 2020; Ghorbani et al., 2019). We study a different setting (extrapolation), and our analysis is non-asymptotic in nature and does not rely on random matrix theory.

Prior works explore GNN extrapolation by testing on larger graphs (Battaglia et al., 2018; Santoro et al., 2018; Saxton et al., 2019; Velickovic et al., 2020). We are the first to theoretically study GNN extrapolation, and we complete the notion of extrapolation to include unseen features and structures.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

We begin by introducing our setting. Let _X_ be the domain of interest, e.g., vectors or graphs. The task is to learn an underlying function _g_ : _X →_ R with a training set _{_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂D]_[,][where] _yi_ = _g_ ( _**x** i_ ) and _D_ is the support of training distribution. Previous works have extensively studied in-distribution generalization where the training and the test distributions are identical (Valiant, 1984; Vapnik, 2013); i.e., _D_ = _X_ . In contrast, extrapolation addresses predictions on a domain _X_ that is larger than the support of the training distribution _D_ . We will say a model _extrapolates well_ if it has a small _extrapolation error_ .

**Definition 1.** (Extrapolation error). Let _f_ : _X →_ R be a model trained on _{_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂D]_[ with] underlying function _g_ : _X →_ R. Let _P_ be a distribution over _X \ D_ and let _ℓ_ : R _×_ R _→_ R be a loss function. We define the extrapolation error of _f_ as E _**x** ∼P_ [ _ℓ_ ( _f_ ( _**x**_ ) _, g_ ( _**x**_ ))].
We focus on neural networks trained by _gradient descent_ (GD) or its variants with _squared loss_ . We study two network architectures: feedforward and graph neural networks.

**Graph Neural Networks.** GNNs are structured networks operating on graphs with MLP modules (Battaglia et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019). Let _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) be a graph. Each node _u ∈ V_ has a feature vector _**x** u_ , and each edge ( _u, v_ ) _∈ E_ has a feature vector _**w**_ ( _u,v_ ). GNNs recursively compute node representations _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] at iteration _k_ (Gilmer et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2018). Initially, _**h**_[(0)] _u_ = _**x** u_ . For _k_ = 1 _..K_ , GNNs update _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] by aggregating the neighbor representations. We can optionally compute a graph representation _**h** G_ by aggregating the final node representations. That is,

The final output is the graph representation _**h** G_ or final node representations _**h**_[(] _u[K]_[)] depending on the task. We refer to the neighbor aggregation step for _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] as _aggregation_ and the pooling step in _**h** G_ as _readout_ . Previous works typically use sum-aggregation and sum-readout (Battaglia et al., 2018). Our results indicate why replacing them may help extrapolation (Section 4).

## 3 HOW FEEDFORWARD NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE

Feedforward networks are the simplest neural networks and building blocks of more complex architectures such as GNNs, so we first study how they extrapolate when trained by GD. Throughout the paper, we assume ReLU activation. Section 3.3 contains preliminary results for other activations.

## 3.1 LINEAR EXTRAPOLATION BEHAVIOR OF RELU MLPS

By architecture, ReLU networks learn piecewise linear functions, but what do these regions precisely look like outside the support of the training data? Figure 1 illustrates examples of how ReLU MLPs extrapolate when trained by GD on various nonlinear functions. These examples suggest that outside the training support, the predictions quickly become linear along directions from the origin. We systematically verify this pattern by linear regression on MLPs’ predictions: the coefficient of determination ( _R_[2] ) is always greater than 0.99 (Appendix C.2). That is, ReLU MLPs “linearize” almost immediately outside the training data range.

We formalize this observation using the implicit biases of neural networks trained by GD via the _neural tangent kernel (NTK)_ : optimization trajectories of over-parameterized networks trained by GD are equivalent to those of kernel regression with a specific neural tangent kernel, under a set of assumptions called the “NTK regime” (Jacot et al., 2018). We provide an informal definition here; for further details, we refer the readers to Jacot et al. (2018) and Appendix A.
**Definition 2.** (Informal) A neural network trained in the _NTK regime_ is infinitely wide, randomly initialized with certain scaling, and trained by GD with infinitesimal steps.

Prior works analyze optimization and in-distribution generalization of over-parameterized neural networks via NTK (Allen-Zhu et al., 2019a;b; Arora et al., 2019a;b; Cao & Gu, 2019; Du et al., 2019c;a; Li & Liang, 2018; Nitanda & Suzuki, 2021). We instead analyze extrapolation.

Theorem 1 formalizes our observation from Figure 1: outside the training data range, along any direction _t_ _**v**_ from the origin, the prediction of a two-layer ReLU MLP quickly converges to a linear

ReLU networks have finitely many linear regions (Arora et al., 2018; Hanin & Rolnick, 2019), hence their predictions _eventually_ become linear. In contrast, Theorem 1 is a more fine-grained analysis of _how_ MLPs extrapolate and provides a convergence rate. While Theorem 1 assumes two-layer networks in the NTK regime, experiments confirm that the linear extrapolation behavior happens across networks with different depths, widths, learning rates, and batch sizes (Appendix C.1 and C.2). Our proof technique potentially also extends to deeper networks.

Theorem 1 implies which target functions a ReLU MLP may be able to match outside the training data: only functions that are almost-linear along the directions away from the origin. While learning linear functions may seem very limited at first, in Section 4 this insight will help explain extrapolation properties of GNNs in non-linear practical tasks. Before that, we first theoretically analyze when MLPs extrapolate well.

## 3.2 WHEN RELU MLPS PROVABLY EXTRAPOLATE WELL

Figure 4a shows that MLPs can extrapolate well when the target function is linear. However, this is not always true. In this section, we show that successful extrapolation depends on the _geometry_ of training data. Intuitively, the training distribution must be “diverse” enough for correct extrapolation.

We provide two conditions that relate the geometry of the training data to extrapolation. Lemma 1 states that over-parameterized MLPs can learn a linear target function with only 2 _d_ examples. **Lemma 1.** _Let g_ ( _**x**_ ) = _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** be the target function for_ _**β** ∈_ R _[d] . Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[contains][an] orthogonal basis {_ _**x**_ ˆ _i}[d] i_ =1 _[and][ {−]_ _**[x]**_[ˆ] _[i][}][d] i_ =1 _[.][If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP][ f][on][ {]_[(] _**[x]**[i][, y][i]_[)] _[}][n] i_ =1 _with squared loss in the NTK regime, then f_ ( _**x**_ ) = _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** for all_ _**x** ∈_ R _[d] ._

Lemma 1 is mainly of theoretical interest, as the 2 _d_ examples need to be carefully chosen. Theorem 2 builds on Lemma 1 and identifies a more practical condition for successful extrapolation: if the support of the training distribution covers all directions (e.g., a hypercube that covers the origin), the MLP converges to a linear target function with sufficient training data.

Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[is sampled from a distribution whose support][ D][contains a connected subset][ S][,] where for any non-zero_ _**w** ∈_ R _[d] , there exists k >_ 0 _so that k_ _**w** ∈S. If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP f_ : R _[d] →_ R _on {_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[with squared loss in the NTK regime,][ f]_[(] _**[x]**_[)] _−→p_ _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** as n →∞._

**Experiments: geometry of training data affects extrapolation.** The condition in Theorem 2 formalizes the intuition that the training distribution must be “diverse” for successful extrapolation, e.g., _D_ includes all directions. Empirically, the extrapolation error is indeed small when the condition of Theorem 2 is satisfied (“all” in Figure 4b). In contrast, the extrapolation error is much larger when the training examples are restricted to only some directions (Figure 4b and Figure 3).

Relating to previous works, Theorem 2 suggests why spurious correlations may hurt extrapolation, complementing the causality arguments (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Peters et al., 2016; Rojas-Carulla et al., 2018). When the training data has spurious correlations, some combinations of features are missing; e.g., camels might only appear in deserts in an image collection. Therefore, the condition for Theorem 2 no longer holds, and the model may extrapolate incorrectly. Theorem 2 is also analogous to an identifiability condition for linear models, but stricter. We can uniquely identify a linear function if the training data has full (feature) rank. MLPs are more expressive, so identifying the linear target function requires additional constraints.

Figure 5: **Extrapolation performance of MLPs with other activation** . MLPs can extrapolate well when the activation is “similar” to the target function. When learning quadratic with quadratic activation, 2-layer networks (quad-2) extrapolate well, but 4-layer networks (quad-4) do not.

To summarize, we analyze how ReLU MLPs extrapolate and provide two insights: (1) MLPs cannot extrapolate most nonlinear tasks due to their linear extrapolation (Theorem 1); and (2) MLPs extrapolate well when the target function is linear, if the training distribution is “diverse” (Theorem 2). In the next section, these results help us understand how more complex networks extrapolate.

## 3.3 MLPS WITH OTHER ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

Before moving on to GNNs, we complete the picture of MLPs with experiments on other activation functions: tanh _σ_ ( _x_ ) = tanh( _x_ ), cosine _σ_ ( _x_ ) = cos( _x_ ) (Lapedes & Farber, 1987; McCaughan, 1997; Sopena & Alquezar, 1994), and quadratic _σ_ ( _x_ ) = _x_[2] (Du & Lee, 2018; Livni et al., 2014). Details are in Appendix C.4. MLPs extrapolate well when the activation and target function are similar; e.g., tanh activation extrapolates well when learning tanh, but not other functions (Figure 5). Moreover, each activation function has different limitations. To extrapolate the tanh function with tanh activation, the training data range has to be sufficiently wide. When learning a quadratic function with quadratic activation, only two-layer networks extrapolate well as more layers lead to higher-order polynomials. Cosine activations are hard to optimize for high-dimensional data, so we only consider one/two dimensional cosine target functions.

## 4 HOW GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE

Above, we saw that extrapolation in nonlinear tasks is hard for MLPs. Despite this limitation, GNNs have been shown to extrapolate well in some nonlinear algorithmic tasks, such as intuitive physics (Battaglia et al., 2016; Janner et al., 2019), graph algorithms (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020), and symbolic mathematics (Lample & Charton, 2020). To address this discrepancy, we build on our MLP results and study how GNNs trained by GD extrapolate.

## 4.1 HYPOTHESIS: LINEAR ALGORITHMIC ALIGNMENT HELPS EXTRAPOLATION

We start with an example: training GNNs to solve the shortest path problem. For this task, prior works observe that a modified GNN architecture with min-aggregation can generalize to graphs larger than those in the training set (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020):
We first provide an intuitive explanation (Figure 2a). Shortest path can be solved by the Bellman-Ford (BF) algorithm (Bellman, 1958) with the following update:

where _**w**_ ( _v, u_ ) is the weight of edge ( _v, u_ ), and _d_ [ _k_ ][ _u_ ] is the shortest distance to node _u_ within _k_ steps. The two equations can be easily aligned: GNNs simulate the BF algorithm if its MLP modules learn a linear function _d_ [ _k −_ 1][ _v_ ]+ _**w**_ ( _v, u_ ). Since MLPs can extrapolate linear tasks, this “alignment” may explain why min-aggregation GNNs can extrapolate well in this task.

For comparison, we can reason why we would not expect GNNs with the more commonly used sum-aggregation (Eqn. 1) to extrapolate well in this task. With sum-aggregation, the MLP modules need to learn a nonlinear function to simulate the BF algorithm, but Theorem 1 suggests that they will not extrapolate most nonlinear functions outside the training support.

We can generalize the above intuition to other algorithmic tasks. Many tasks where GNNs extrapolate well can be solved by dynamic programming (DP) (Bellman, 1966), an algorithmic paradigm with a recursive structure similar to GNNs’ (Eqn. 1) (Xu et al., 2020).

**Definition 3.** _Dynamic programming_ (DP) is a recursive procedure with updates

where Answer[ _k_ ][ _s_ ] is the solution to a sub-problem indexed by iteration _k_ and state _s_ , and DP-Update is a task-specific update function that solves the sub-problem based on the previous iteration.

From a broader standpoint, we hypothesize that: if we encode appropriate non-linearities into the model architecture and input representations so that the MLP modules only need to learn nearly linear steps, then the resulting neural network can extrapolate well.

> **Hypothesis 1.** (Linear algorithmic alignment). Let _f_ : _X →_ R be the underlying function and _N_ a neural network with _m_ MLP modules. Suppose there exist _m linear_ functions _{gi}[m] i_ =1[so that by] replacing _N_ ’s MLP modules with _gi_ ’s, _N_ simulates _f_ . Given _ϵ >_ 0, there exists _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂] D_ ⊊ _X_ so that _N_ trained on _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1[by GD with squared loss learns] _[f]_[ˆ][with] _[ ∥][f]_[ˆ] _[−][f][∥][< ϵ]_[.]

Our hypothesis builds on the algorithmic alignment framework of (Xu et al., 2020), which states that a neural network _interpolates_ well if the modules are “aligned” to easy-to-learn (possibly nonlinear) functions. Successful extrapolation is harder: the modules need to align with linear functions.

**Applications of linear algorithmic alignment.** In general, linear algorithmic alignment is not restricted to GNNs and applies broadly to neural networks. To satisfy the condition, we can encode appropriate nonlinear operations in the _architecture_ or _input representation_ (Figure 2). Learning DP algorithms with GNNs is one example of encoding non-linearity in the architecture (Battaglia et al., 2018; Corso et al., 2020). Another example is to encode log-and-exp transforms in the architecture to help extrapolate multiplication in arithmetic tasks (Trask et al., 2018; Madsen & Johansen, 2020). Neural symbolic programs take a step further and encode a library of symbolic operations to help extrapolation (Johnson et al., 2017; Mao et al., 2019; Yi et al., 2018).

For some tasks, it may be easier to change the input representation (Figure 2b). Sometimes, we can decompose the target function _f_ as _f_ = _g ◦ h_ into a feature embedding _h_ and a “simpler” target function _g_ that our model can extrapolate well. We can obtain _h_ via specialized features or feature transforms using _domain knowledge_ (Lample & Charton, 2020; Webb et al., 2020), or via _representation learning_ (e.g., BERT) with unlabeled out-of-distribution data in _X \ D_ (Chen et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020; Mikolov et al., 2013b; Peters et al., 2018). This brings a new perspective of how representations help extrapolation in various application areas. For example, in natural language processing, pretrained representations (Mikolov et al., 2013a; Wu & Dredze, 2019) and feature transformation using domain knowledge (Yuan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019) help models generalize across languages, a special type of extrapolation. In quantitative finance, identifying the right “factors” or features is crucial for deep learning models as the financial markets may frequently be in extrapolation regimes (Banz, 1981; Fama & French, 1993; Ross, 1976).

Linear algorithmic alignment explains successful extrapolation in the literature and suggests that extrapolation is harder in general: encoding appropriate non-linearity often requires domain expertise or model search. Next, we provide theoretical and empirical support for our hypothesis.

## 4.2 THEORETICAL AND EMPIRICAL SUPPORT

We validate our hypothesis on three DP tasks: max degree, shortest path, and _n_ -body problem, and prove the hypothesis for max degree. We highlight the role of graph structures in extrapolation.

**Theoretical analysis.** We start with a simple yet fundamental task: learning the max degree of a graph, a special case of DP with one iteration. As a corollary of Theorem 1, the commonly used sum-based GNN (Eqn. 1) cannot extrapolate well (proof in Appendix B.4).

To achieve linear algorithmic alignment, we can encode the only non-linearity, the max function, in the readout. Theorem 3 confirms that a GNN with max-readout can extrapolate well in this task.

Theorem 3 does not follow immediately from Theorem 2, because MLP modules in GNNs only receive indirect supervision. We analyze the _Graph NTK_ (Du et al., 2019b) to prove Theorem 3 in Appendix B.5. While Theorem 3 assumes identical node features, we empirically observe similar results for both identical and non-identical features (Figure 16 in Appendix).

**Interpretation of conditions.** The condition in Theorem 3 is analogous to that in Theorem 2. Both theorems require diverse training data, measured by _graph structure_ in Theorem 3 or _directions_ in Theorem 2. In Theorem 3, the condition is violated if all training graphs have the same max or min node degrees, e.g., when training data are from one of the following families: path, _C_ -regular graphs (regular graphs with degree _C_ ), cycle, and ladder.

**Experiments: architectures that help extrapolation.** We validate our theoretical analysis with two DP tasks: max degree and shortest path (details in Appendix C.5 and C.6). While previous works only test on graphs with different sizes (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020), we also test on graphs with unseen structure, edge weights and node features. The results support our theory. For max degree, GNNs with max-readout are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a), confirming Corollary 1 and Theorem 3. For shortest path, GNNs with min-readout and min-aggregation are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a).

Experiments confirm the importance of training graphs structure (Figure 7). Interestingly, the two tasks favor different graph structure. For max degree, as Theorem 3 predicts, GNNs extrapolate well when trained on trees, complete graphs, expanders, and general graphs, and extrapolation errors are

For shortest path, extrapolation errors follow a U-shaped curve as we change the _sparsity_ of training graphs (Figure 7b and Figure 18 in Appendix). Intuitively, models trained on sparse or dense graphs likely learn degenerative solutions.

**Experiments: representations that help extrapolation.** Finally, we show a good input representation helps extrapolation. We study the _n_ -body problem (Battaglia et al., 2016; Watters et al., 2017) (Appendix C.7), that is, predicting the time evolution of _n_ objects in a gravitational system. ## 5 CONNECTIONS TO OTHER OUT-OF-DISTRIBUTION SETTINGS

We discuss several related settings. Intuitively, from the viewpoint of our results above, methods in related settings may improve extrapolation by 1) learning useful non-linearities beyond the training data range and 2) mapping relevant test data to the training data range.

**Domain adaptation** studies generalization to a specific target domain (Ben-David et al., 2010; Blitzer et al., 2008; Mansour et al., 2009). Typical strategies adjust the training process: for instance, use unlabeled samples from the target domain to align the target and source distributions (Ganin et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). Using target domain data during training may induce useful non-linearities and may mitigate extrapolation by matching the target and source distributions, though the correctness of the learned mapping depends on the label distribution (Zhao et al., 2019).

**Self-supervised learning** on a large amount of unlabeled data can learn useful non-linearities beyond the labeled training data range (Chen et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Peters et al., 2018). Hence, our results suggest an explanation why pre-trained representations such as BERT improve out-of-distribution robustness (Hendrycks et al., 2020). In addition, self-supervised learning could map semantically similar data to similar representations, so some out-of-domain examples might fall inside the training distribution after the mapping.

**Invariant models** aim to learn features that respect specific invariances across multiple training distributions (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Rojas-Carulla et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). If the model indeed learns these invariances, which can happen in the linear case and when there are confounders or anti-causal variables (Ahuja et al., 2021; Rosenfeld et al., 2021), this may essentially increase the training data range, since variations in the invariant features may be ignored by the model.

**Distributional robustness** considers small adversarial perturbations of the data distribution, and ensures that the model performs well under these (Goh & Sim, 2010; Sagawa et al., 2020; Sinha et al., 2018; Staib & Jegelka, 2019). We instead look at more global perturbations. Still, one would expect that modifications that help extrapolation in general also improve robustness to local perturbations.

## 6 CONCLUSION

This paper is an initial step towards formally understanding how neural networks trained by gradient descent extrapolate. We identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate as desired. We also suggest an explanation how GNNs have been able to extrapolate well in complex algorithmic tasks: encoding appropriate non-linearity in architecture and features can help extrapolation. Our results and hypothesis agree with empirical results, in this paper and in the literature.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a valuable contribution by offering a theoretical framework to explain why GNNs can extrapolate in certain algorithmic tasks while standard MLPs struggle. It effectively connects the implicit bias of gradient descent in over-parameterized networks to extrapolation behavior, leveraging the NTK regime to formalize intuitions about directional linearity. The hypothesis of 'linear algorithmic alignment' is insightful and helps unify disparate empirical findings in the literature.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient empirical validation of theoretical claims in realistic GNN settings: The theoretical results for GNNs, particularly Theorem 3 on max degree extrapolation, are supported by experiments on highly simplified tasks that do not reflect the complexity of real-world graph learning. The empirical evaluation uses synthetic graphs (e.g., ER, regular, trees) with hand-designed structural variations, but does not test on standard benchmark datasets or more complex algorithmic tasks where size generalization is nontrivial. This weakens the claim that the theory explains real extrapolation successes. For example, while the paper cites works on shortest paths and n-body problems, its validation is limited to toy versions. A more convincing demonstration would include evaluation on established benchmarks like those in OOD-GNN or follow-up studies that test size generalization on larger, more diverse graph families.

- Over-reliance on the NTK regime without addressing finite-width effects: The core theoretical results (Theorems 1–3) assume the infinite-width NTK regime, yet the paper generalizes these findings to practical finite-width networks without sufficient empirical or theoretical justification. While Appendix C.1 and C.2 show similar behavior across architectures, there is no systematic ablation showing how width affects extrapolation error or whether the linearization rate degrades with smaller widths. Given that real GNNs operate far from the infinite-width limit, the applicability of the theory is questionable. The paper should either provide a finite-width extension of the analysis or include a controlled study measuring deviation from NTK predictions as width decreases.

- Lack of ablation on architectural components critical to extrapolation: The paper attributes successful extrapolation to encoding task-specific non-linearities (e.g., min-aggregation), but does not isolate the contribution of individual architectural choices. For instance, in the shortest path task, it is unclear whether performance gains come from min-aggregation, the MLP's ability to learn offsets, or interaction effects. A proper ablation would compare min-aggregation against sum-aggregation with transformed inputs or auxiliary supervision. Without such analysis, the evidence for 'linear algorithmic alignment' remains correlational rather than causal. The same applies to the n-body experiment, where the improved features may help for reasons beyond linearity, such as better conditioning.

- Ambiguous definition of extrapolation across domains: The paper treats 'extrapolation' as a single concept but applies it to different types of out-of-distribution shifts: input magnitude (MLPs), graph size (GNNs), edge weights, and node features. These shifts have different geometric and statistical properties, yet the theory is presented as universally applicable. For example, Theorem 2 assumes a continuous input space with radial directions, which does not directly map to discrete graph structures where 'direction' is ill-defined. This conflation risks overgeneralization. The paper should clarify whether the NTK-based directional linearity argument extends meaningfully to discrete combinatorial domains, or restrict the scope accordingly.

- Underdeveloped discussion of limitations and failure modes: The paper emphasizes conditions under which extrapolation succeeds but gives little attention to known failure modes of GNNs, such as over-squashing or inability to count, which are well-documented in the literature. For instance, even with min-aggregation, GNNs may fail to extrapolate shortest paths on very long chains due to bottlenecked information flow. The discussion in Section 5 touches on related ideas but does not engage with these architectural limits. Acknowledging such boundaries would strengthen the paper’s credibility and help position its contributions more precisely.

- No simulation verifying MLP linearization rate: The paper claims in Theorem 1 that ReLU MLPs converge to linear functions along radial directions at rate $O(1/t)$, but provides no simulation to verify this rate. Readers will wonder whether the theoretical rate matches empirical observations, especially near the training boundary where extrapolation begins. To support the claim, the authors should simulate a two-layer ReLU network in the NTK regime on a simple nonlinear target (e.g., quadratic in 2D), compute the directional derivative along a ray $t\mathbf{v}$ for increasing $t$, and plot the deviation from linearity against $1/t$ to confirm the predicted convergence slope.

- Missing worked example for Graph NTK in Theorem 3: Theorem 3 uses Graph NTK to prove extrapolation for max degree with max-readout, but the paper does not compute any explicit Graph NTK values or training dynamics for a concrete graph family. This makes it hard to assess whether the NTK framework meaningfully captures the learning behavior in this discrete-structure setting. The authors should compute the Graph NTK for a pair of small graphs (e.g., a 3-node path vs. a triangle) under the max-readout architecture, show how the kernel reflects structural similarity, and verify that gradient descent converges to the correct max degree function under the spanning condition.

- No comparison to alternative extrapolation mechanisms: The paper attributes GNN extrapolation success solely to linear algorithmic alignment via encoded non-linearities, but does not compare against other known mechanisms like position-aware architectures or virtual nodes that also improve size generalization. A reader might ask whether these alternatives align with the same principle or contradict it. The authors should test a GNN with global attention or virtual nodes on the max degree task and report whether it extrapolates well despite not using max-readout, then discuss how such architectures fit (or challenge) their hypothesis.

- No guidance on detecting alignment in practice: The central hypothesis depends on whether a GNN’s MLP modules learn nearly linear functions during extrapolation, but the paper offers no practical method to diagnose this in trained models. For the claim to be useful, practitioners need a way to assess alignment post-hoc. The authors should propose a diagnostic—such as fitting linear probes to MLP outputs on test graphs or measuring input-output correlation—and apply it to their shortest path experiments to show that min-aggregation leads to more linear module behavior than sum-aggregation.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct a finite-width ablation study showing how extrapolation behavior changes with network width, and discuss implications for the NTK assumption

- Expand empirical validation to include standard OOD-GNN benchmarks or more complex graph algorithm tasks with established size generalization challenges

- Perform controlled ablations isolating the effect of specific architectural components (e.g., aggregation type, input transformation) on extrapolation performance

- Clarify the definition of extrapolation across continuous and discrete domains, and qualify the extent to which the NTK analysis applies to graph-structured inputs

- Discuss known architectural limitations of GNNs (e.g., over-squashing, counting) and how they interact with the proposed linear alignment hypothesis

comments.title: Ambiguous extrapolation definition across domains
comments.quote: We study how neural networks trained by gradient descent *extrapolate*, i.e., what they learn outside the support of the training distribution. Previous works report mixed empirical results when extrapolating with neural networks: while feedforward neural networks, a.k.a. multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), do not extrapolate well in certain simple tasks, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) – structured networks with MLP modules – have shown some success in more complex tasks.
comments.explanation: The term 'extrapolate' is used uniformly across continuous input spaces and discrete graph structures, but the geometric assumptions differ fundamentally. For MLPs, extrapolation is analyzed along rays in $\mathbb{R}^d$, relying on directional continuity and scaling. For GNNs, 'extrapolation' includes shifts in graph size, edge weights, and structure—discrete changes where 'direction from the origin' lacks meaning. The paper does not clarify how the NTK-based directional linearity argument extends to such combinatorial shifts. This conflation risks overgeneralizing the theory. The definition should either restrict the scope to continuous domains or explicitly address how the NTK framework adapts to discrete structural changes.

comments.title: Graph NTK not mentioned despite theoretical reliance
comments.quote: We study two network architectures: feedforward and graph neural networks.
comments.explanation: The paper relies on Graph NTK for Theorem 3 but introduces no distinction between standard NTK and its graph-structured extension. The Graph NTK involves Jacobian analysis over graph parameters, permutation invariance, and assumptions on degree distributions—substantially more complex than the feedforward case. By omitting any mention of Graph NTK in the preliminaries, the paper implies a uniform theoretical treatment that obscures key assumptions. Since the GNN result depends on this framework, it should be explicitly named and contextualized to avoid misleading readers about the analysis's scope and validity.

comments.title: Sum-readout equation misaligned with theoretical assumptions
comments.quote: quad\bm{h}_{G}=\text{MLP}^{(K+1)}\Big{(}\sum_{u\in G}\bm{h}_{u}^{(K)}\Big{)}.$ (1)
comments.explanation: The readout equation applies an MLP to the sum of node representations, but does not specify whether this MLP is linear. For Theorem 3 and Corollary 1 to hold—where sum-based GNNs fail to extrapolate linear functions—the readout must preserve linearity. If $\text{MLP}^{(K+1)}$ contains ReLU activations, the output may saturate or grow sublinearly, breaking extrapolation even if the sum scales linearly. The intended analysis likely assumes a linear readout, a common simplification in NTK studies, but this is not stated. Without clarification, the equation contradicts the paper’s own theoretical claims.

comments.title: No handling of isolated nodes in GNN update rule
comments.quote: $\bm{h}_{u}^{(k)}=\sum_{v\in\mathcal{N}(u)}\text{MLP}^{(k)}\Big{(}\bm{h}_{u}^{(k-1)},\bm{h}_{v}^{(k-1)},\bm{w}_{(v,u)}\Big{)},\quad\bm{h}_{G}=\text{MLP}^{(K+1)}\Big{(}\sum_{u\in G}\bm{h}_{u}^{(K)}\Big{)}.$ (1)
comments.explanation: The update rule sums over $\mathcal{N}(u)$, but does not define behavior when $\mathcal{N}(u) = \emptyset$. Under standard interpretation, $\bm{h}_{u}^{(k)} = 0$, which collapses the representation of isolated nodes after one step. This contradicts the initialization $\bm{h}_{u}^{(0)} = \bm{x}_{u}$ and risks failure on graphs with isolated nodes. Standard GNNs avoid this via self-loops or residual connections. The absence of such a mechanism creates a gap between the stated model and practical implementations, especially for extrapolation to unseen structures.

comments.title: Ambiguity in MLP form within GNN message function
comments.quote: $\bm{h}_{u}^{(k)}=\sum_{v\in\mathcal{N}(u)}\text{MLP}^{(k)}\Big{(}\bm{h}_{u}^{(k-1)},\bm{h}_{v}^{(k-1)},\bm{w}_{(v,u)}\Big{)},\quad\bm{h}_{G}=\text{MLP}^{(K+1)}\Big{(}\sum_{u\in G}\bm{h}_{u}^{(K)}\Big{)}.$ (1)
comments.explanation: The notation suggests the MLP is applied per edge before summation, but this is not explicit. More critically, the paper does not clarify whether $\text{MLP}^{(k)}$ is a standard nonlinear MLP or its NTK-linearized version. For Theorem 3 to hold, the analysis must assume the infinite-width limit where the MLP behaves linearly. Without stating this, the connection to the NTK framework is unjustified. The message function should be distinguished from the finite-width implementation to avoid confusion between architecture and training dynamics.

comments.title: Extrapolation error definition lacks domain specification
comments.quote: We define the extrapolation error of $f$ as $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}\sim\mathcal{P}}[\ell(f(\bm{x}),g(\bm{x}))]$.
comments.explanation: The definition depends on a distribution $\mathcal{P}$ over $\mathcal{X}\setminus\mathcal{D}$, but no constraints are placed on $\mathcal{P}$. Without requiring $\mathcal{P}$ to include rays $t\nu$ as $t \to \infty$, the error may reflect behavior in irrelevant directions. Theorem 2 only guarantees good extrapolation when the training support spans all directions, implying that $\mathcal{P}$ must probe such directions to validate the result. A minimal fix is to require that $\mathcal{P}$ includes asymptotic rays in relevant directions, aligning the definition with the directional analysis in the theory.

comments.title: Theorem 1 omits explicit convergence rate
comments.quote:
Theorem 1 formalizes our observation from Figure 1: outside the training data range, along any direction  $t\nu$  from the origin, the prediction of a two-layer ReLU MLP quickly converges to a linear

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

function with rate $O(\frac{1}{t})$. The linear coefficients $\bm{\beta_{v}}$ and the constant terms in the convergence rate depend on the training data and direction $\bm{v}$. The proof is in Appendix B.1.
comments.explanation: The main text claims a convergence rate of $O(1/t)$, but the formal statement of Theorem 1 does not include this rate. Instead, it states that for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $t = O(1/\epsilon)$ such that the difference quotient is within $\epsilon$ of $\beta_{\bm{v}}$. This implies $ \left| \frac{f(t\bm{v} + h\bm{v}) - f(t\bm{v})}{h} - \beta_{\bm{v}} \right| = O(1/t) $, but this asymptotic bound is not explicitly stated. To ensure clarity, the rate should be part of the theorem’s conclusion, as readers cannot extract it without reconstructing the logic.

comments.title: Directional convergence lacks uniformity
comments.quote: As $t\rightarrow\infty$, $f(\bm{x}_{0}+h\bm{v})-f(\bm{x}_{0})\rightarrow\beta_{\bm{v}}\cdot h$ for any $h>0$, where $\beta_{\bm{v}}$ is a constant linear coefficient.
comments.explanation: The convergence statement does not specify whether $\beta_{\bm{v}}$ depends on $h$ or whether the limit holds uniformly. For asymptotic linearity, the difference quotient $ \frac{f(t\bm{v} + h\bm{v}) - f(t\bm{v})}{h} $ must converge to a fixed $\beta_{\bm{v}}$ independent of $h$, and uniformly for $h$ in compact intervals. Otherwise, $h$-dependent limits could define nonlinear behavior. The correct formulation should ensure the slope stabilizes along the ray, which requires uniform convergence over $h$.

comments.title: Ambiguous ray condition in Theorem 2
comments.quote: Suppose $\{\bm{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$ is sampled from a distribution whose support $\mathcal{D}$ contains a connected subset $\mathcal{S}$, where for any non-zero $\bm{w}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$, there exists $k>0$ so that $k\bm{w}\in\mathcal{S}$.
comments.explanation: The condition allows sets like a spherical shell outside the unit ball, which satisfies the ray requirement but contains no neighborhood of the origin. In the NTK regime, gradient updates depend on local variation near initialization. Without data near zero, the network may fail to identify the correct linear function, even if directional coverage is satisfied. A sufficient condition would be that $\mathcal{S}$ contains an open ball around the origin. The current phrasing permits sets insufficient for identifiability under gradient flow.

comments.title: Incorrect convergence mode in Theorem 2
comments.quote: If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP $f:\mathbb{R}^{d}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ on $\{(\bm{x}_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with squared loss in the NTK regime, $f(\bm{x})\overset{p}{\longrightarrow}\bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{x}$ as $n\rightarrow\infty$.
comments.explanation: In the NTK regime, convergence is typically uniform on compact sets, not pointwise over $\mathbb{R}^d$. Pointwise convergence in probability does not control behavior on unbounded domains, which is essential for extrapolation. The standard result shows convergence to the kernel ridge regression solution uniformly on compacts. Since extrapolation occurs far from the origin, the paper should state uniform convergence on compact subsets to reflect the actual NTK theory and support meaningful extrapolation claims.

comments.title: Training set lacks min degree labels for spanning condition
comments.quote: Let  $g$  and  $g'$  be the max/min degree function, respectively. Let  $\{(G_i, g(G_i))_{i=1}^n\}$  be the training set. If  $\{(g(G_i), g'(G_i), g(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\max}, g'(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\min})\}_{i=1}^n$  spans  $\mathbb{R}^4$ , where  $N_i^{\max}$  and  $N_i^{\min}$  are the number of nodes that have max/min degree on  $G_i$ , then one-layer max-readout GNNs trained on  $\{(G_i, g(G_i))_{i=1}^n\}$  with squared loss in the NTK regime learn  $g$ .
comments.explanation: The spanning condition depends on $g'(G_i)$, the min degree, which is not part of the training labels. Since the model is only supervised on max degree, it cannot access $g'(G_i)$ or $N_i^{\min}$ during training. Two graphs with the same max degree but different min degrees cannot be distinguished in the feature space without this information. The condition thus relies on unobserved quantities, creating a logical gap between supervision and identifiability.

comments.title: Unjustified appearance of $N_i^{\max}$ in NTK feature map
comments.quote: If  $\{(g(G_i), g'(G_i), g(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\max}, g'(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\min})\}_{i=1}^n$  spans  $\mathbb{R}^4$ , where  $N_i^{\max}$  and  $N_i^{\min}$  are the number of nodes that have max/min degree on  $G_i$ , then one-layer max-readout GNNs trained on  $\{(G_i, g(G_i))_{i=1}^n\}$  with squared loss in the NTK regime learn  $g$ .
comments.explanation: The term $g(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\max}$ appears in the spanning condition, but $N_i^{\max}$ is a count of nodes achieving max degree. In a one-layer max-readout GNN, the output depends only on the max value, not how many nodes achieve it. The NTK feature map for max-readout depends on which node activates, not multiplicity. Without a derivation showing $N_i^{\max}$ is encoded in the Jacobian or kernel, its inclusion in the condition lacks justification and appears unmotivated.

comments.title: Extrapolation claim not verified for non-identical features
comments.quote: While Theorem 3 assumes identical node features, we empirically observe similar results for both identical and non-identical features (Figure 16 in Appendix).
comments.explanation: Theorem 3 is proven only for identical features, but the paper claims empirical support for non-identical features without theoretical justification. When node features vary, max-readout may not reflect max degree if features encode non-structural information. The $n$-body experiment uses non-identical features but relies on transformed edge representations—a different mechanism. Thus, the generalization to non-identical features lacks both theoretical and direct empirical support in the max degree task.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of paper's theoretical scope
comments.quote: This paper is an initial step towards formally understanding how neural networks trained by gradient descent extrapolate. We identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate as desired.
comments.explanation: The claim implies equal theoretical grounding for MLPs and GNNs, but only Theorem 3 provides formal analysis for GNNs—and it applies narrowly to max degree with max-readout and identical features. The broader claim about GNNs in complex tasks lacks a corresponding theorem. The conclusion should reflect the disparity: rigorous conditions for MLPs, and a theoretical explanation in a simplified GNN task supported by empirical evidence in more complex settings.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about explanation for GNN success
comments.quote: We also suggest an explanation how GNNs have been able to extrapolate well in complex algorithmic tasks: encoding appropriate non-linearity in architecture and features can help extrapolation.
comments.explanation: The paper presents the hypothesis as an established explanation, but only proves it for max degree. For shortest path and $n$-body, success is shown empirically without proving that linear algorithmic alignment is the causal mechanism. The paper shows correlation, not causation. The claim should be reframed as a hypothesis with partial support rather than a general explanation for success in complex tasks.

comments.title: Overstatement of agreement with literature
comments.quote: Our results and hypothesis agree with empirical results, in this paper and in the literature.
comments.explanation: The claim of broad agreement overlooks known failure modes of GNNs—such as over-squashing, long-range dependencies, and counting tasks—documented in the literature. Asserting general alignment without acknowledging contradictory evidence risks misrepresenting the field. The statement should clarify that the hypothesis explains *some* successes (e.g., size generalization in shortest paths) but does not account for all observed behaviors.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a theoretical and empirical analysis of neural network extrapolation, focusing on ReLU MLPs and GNNs. The main contributions are: (1) proving that ReLU MLPs converge to linear functions outside the training support with rate O(1/t), (2) identifying conditions for successful extrapolation in linear tasks, and (3) hypothesizing and providing evidence that GNNs extrapolate well in algorithmic tasks when appropriate non-linearities are encoded in architecture or features. The work connects feedforward and GNN extrapolation mechanisms through the lens of linear algorithmic alignment.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by providing the first formal theoretical analysis of extrapolation in neural networks. The connection between MLP linearization and GNN success in algorithmic tasks is insightful. The theoretical results are technically sound and the empirical validation supports the claims. However, the paper has significant limitations in scope and generalizability that need to be addressed.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical analysis limited to ReLU activation and NTK regime: The paper's main theoretical results (Theorems 1-3) rely heavily on ReLU activation and the NTK regime assumptions. While Section 3.3 briefly mentions other activations, the theoretical framework doesn't extend beyond ReLU. The NTK regime (infinitely wide networks, infinitesimal learning rates) is a strong assumption that may not hold in practice. The paper needs to clarify how these limitations affect the generalizability of the results to practical settings where networks are finite and trained with standard optimization. A discussion of how the results might extend beyond these assumptions would strengthen the contribution.

- Empirical validation uses only synthetic algorithmic tasks: The empirical validation in Section 4 focuses exclusively on synthetic algorithmic tasks (max degree, shortest path, n-body problem). While these tasks demonstrate the theoretical principles, they don't address whether the findings generalize to real-world graph applications. The literature context mentions works on OOD generalization for real-world graphs, but this paper doesn't test its hypothesis on such datasets. Adding experiments on at least one real-world graph dataset with extrapolation challenges would make the claims more compelling and show practical relevance beyond toy algorithmic problems.

- Insufficient analysis of graph heterophily impact: The paper's analysis of GNN extrapolation doesn't adequately address how graph heterophily (where connected nodes have different features/labels) affects extrapolation performance. The theoretical analysis (Theorem 3) assumes identical node features, which is unrealistic for many applications. While the appendix mentions empirical results with non-identical features, there's no systematic analysis of how heterophily impacts the linear algorithmic alignment hypothesis. Given that heterophily is known to challenge GNN performance, this is a significant oversight that limits the applicability of the results to diverse graph structures.

- Missing discussion of over-squashing limitations: The literature context identifies over-squashing as a key limitation in GNNs that affects long-range dependencies, but the paper doesn't address how this interacts with extrapolation. The hypothesis about encoding non-linearities in architecture doesn't consider that even with proper non-linearity encoding, over-squashing could still limit extrapolation to distant nodes in large graphs. The paper should discuss whether over-squashing fundamentally limits extrapolation regardless of architectural choices, or if the proposed solutions implicitly address this issue.

- Ambiguous connection between extrapolation and generalization: The paper implicitly assumes that better extrapolation implies better generalization, but doesn't explicitly analyze this relationship. The calibration notes flag this as a potential issue. The paper should clarify whether extrapolation ability on algorithmic tasks correlates with generalization performance on more complex, non-algorithmic tasks. This connection is crucial for understanding the practical implications of the findings and avoiding the misconception that extrapolation is always desirable or sufficient for good generalization.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous theoretical result: The paper's main theoretical results (Theoremsms 1-3) need concrete demonstrations that they are non-vacuous. For Theorem 1, the paper should provide a specific nonlinear target function (e.g., quadratic x^T A x) and explicitly compute the linear coefficients β_v that the MLP converges to, showing how this leads to poor extrapolation. For Theorem 2, the paper should construct a specific training distribution that violates the 'diverse' condition (e.g., constrained to positive orthant) and demonstrate how this causes extrapolation failure even for linear targets. Without these concrete examples, readers cannot gauge the practical significance of the theoretical conditions.

- No worked special case for Graph NTK analysis: The Graph NTK proof for Theorem 3 lacks a concrete worked example. The paper should compute the Graph NTK explicitly for a simple graph family (e.g., path graphs of varying lengths) and show how the condition about spanning R^4 translates to specific graph properties. This would help readers understand why identical node features are assumed and how the theorem's conditions connect to observable graph statistics. A worked computation would bridge the gap between the abstract NTK analysis and the empirical results on max degree.

- Underdeveloped practical guidance for encoding non-linearities: The paper claims that encoding appropriate non-linearities in architecture or features enables extrapolation, but provides minimal guidance on how to identify these non-linearities for new tasks. Beyond the three DP tasks studied, the paper should include at least one additional worked example showing how to decompose a new algorithmic task into linear and non-linear components. For instance, analyze PageRank or graph clustering to demonstrate how the linear algorithmic alignment principle guides architectural choices. Without this, the hypothesis remains abstract and difficult to apply to novel problems.

- Missing comparison to baseline extrapolation methods: The paper positions its approach against general GNN architectures but doesn't compare against methods specifically designed for extrapolation. For completeness, the empirical validation should include comparisons to: (1) explicit kernel methods using the same NTK analysis, (2) symbolic regression approaches that directly search for algorithmic structure, and (3) recent meta-learning methods for OOD generalization. This would clarify whether the architectural encoding of non-linearities provides unique benefits beyond what's achievable with existing extrapolation techniques. The current comparisons are insufficient to establish the necessity of the proposed approach.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Extend empirical validation to include at least one real-world graph dataset with extrapolation challenges

- Provide a systematic analysis of how graph heterophily affects the linear algorithmic alignment hypothesis

- Discuss the implications of over-squashing on extrapolation limits and how it interacts with the proposed architectural solutions

- Clarify the relationship between extrapolation ability and generalization performance in non-algorithmic tasks

- Expand the theoretical discussion beyond ReLU activation and NTK regime to address practical training scenarios

comments.title: Abstract overstates theoretical coverage
comments.quote: Working towards a theoretical explanation, we identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate well. First, we quantify the observation that ReLU MLPs quickly converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin, which implies that ReLU MLPs do not extrapolate most nonlinear functions. But, they can provably learn a linear target function when the training distribution is sufficiently “diverse”. Second, in connection to analyzing the successes and limitations of GNNs, these results suggest a hypothesis for which we provide theoretical and empirical evidence: the success of GNNs in extrapolating algorithmic tasks to new data (e.g., larger graphs or edge weights) relies on encoding task-specific non-linearities in the architecture or features.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims to provide theoretical evidence for the GNN extrapolation hypothesis, but Theorem 3 only proves this for the simplified max degree task under restrictive assumptions (identical node features, specific spanning condition). The abstract should clarify that theoretical evidence is provided only for a simplified case, not the general hypothesis across all algorithmic tasks. Rewrite "for which we provide theoretical and empirical evidence" as "for which we provide theoretical evidence in a simplified case and empirical evidence more broadly" to accurately reflect the paper's contributions.

comments.title: Overgeneralization about GNN extrapolation
comments.quote: We show GNNs with appropriate architecture, input representation, and training distribution can predict well on graphs with unseen sizes, structures, edge weights, and node features.
comments.explanation: This claim overgeneralizes the empirical results. The empirical validation only covers three specific DP tasks (max degree, shortest paths, and n-body problem). The claim suggests GNNs can extrapolate across all these dimensions universally, but Theorem 3 shows that extrapolation fails for certain graph families like paths, regular graphs, cycles, and ladders. The introduction should qualify this claim to reflect the specific conditions and limitations demonstrated in the technical sections.

comments.title: Theorem 1 statement has mathematical inconsistency
comments.quote: For any direction $\bm{v}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$, let $\bm{x}_{0}=t\bm{v}$. As $t\rightarrow\infty$, $f(\bm{x}_{0}+h\bm{v})-f(\bm{x}_{0})\rightarrow\beta_{\bm{v}}\cdot h$ for any $h>0$, where $\beta_{\bm{v}}$ is a constant linear coefficient. Moreover, given $\epsilon>0$, for $t=O(\frac{1}{\epsilon})$, we have $|\frac{f(\bm{x}_{0}+h\bm{v})-f(\bm{x}_{0})}{h}-\beta_{\bm{v}}|<\epsilon$.
comments.explanation: The notation $t=O(\frac{1}{\epsilon})$ is mathematically inconsistent with the intended meaning. Big-O notation describes asymptotic upper bounds, but here it's used to relate $t$ and $\epsilon$ in a way that suggests $t$ is bounded above by $C/\epsilon$. For convergence as $t \to \infty$, we need $t$ to be bounded below. Rewrite the convergence rate statement as "Moreover, given $\epsilon>0$, there exists a constant $C>0$ such that for all $t \geq C/\epsilon$, we have $|\frac{f(\bm{x}_{0}+h\bm{v})-f(\bm{x}_{0})}{h}-\beta_{\bm{v}}|<\epsilon$" to correctly capture the asymptotic convergence behavior.

comments.title: Theorem 2 condition insufficient for convergence
comments.quote: Suppose $\{\bm{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$ is sampled from a distribution whose support $\mathcal{D}$ contains a connected subset $\mathcal{S}$, where for any non-zero $\bm{w}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$, there exists $k>0$ so that $k\bm{w}\in\mathcal{S}$. If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP $f:\mathbb{R}^{d}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ on $\{(\bm{x}_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with squared loss in the NTK regime, $f(\bm{x})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}\bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{x}$ as $n\rightarrow\infty$.
comments.explanation: The condition in Theorem 2 is insufficient to guarantee convergence to the linear target function. The requirement that $\mathcal{S}$ contains points in all directions does not ensure that the training data spans $\mathbb{R}^d$ in the linear algebra sense. Consider $\mathcal{S}$ being the union of $d$ one-dimensional rays from the origin along each coordinate axis. This satisfies the condition but all training points lie in a $d$-dimensional subspace of measure zero, making the linear function non-identifiable. The NTK matrix would be singular, preventing convergence. Rewrite the condition as requiring that $\mathcal{S}$ contains an open set in $\mathbb{R}^d$ or has non-zero Lebesgue measure to ensure the NTK matrix is invertible.

comments.title: Theorem 3 condition insufficient for spanning R^4
comments.quote: If  $\{(g(G_i), g'(G_i), g(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\max}, g'(G_i) \cdot N_i^{\min})\}_{i=1}^n$  spans  $\mathbb{R}^4$ , where  $N_i^{\max}$  and  $N_i^{\min}$  are the number of nodes that have max/min degree on  $G_i$ , then one-layer max-readout GNNs trained on  $\{(G_i, g(G_i))\}_{i=1}^n$  with squared loss in the NTK regime learn  $g$ .
comments.explanation: The spanning condition is problematic due to inherent constraints between the four quantities. For any graph, we have g(G_i) ≥ g'(G_i) and N_i^max, N_i^min ≥ 1. More critically, the products g(G_i)·N_i^max and g'(G_i)·N_i^min are not independent of g(G_i) and g'(G_i) - they are scalar multiples. The four-dimensional vector space cannot be spanned when two coordinates are scalar multiples of the other two. The condition should be reformulated to require that the set {(g(G_i), g'(G_i), N_i^max, N_i^min)} spans R^4, or more precisely, that the training graphs provide sufficient variation in these four independent quantities.

comments.title: GNN aggregation equation missing self-loop
comments.quote: $$\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k)}=\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}(u)} \operatorname{MLP}^{(k)}\left(\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{v}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{w}_{(v, u)}\right), \quad \mathbf{h}_{G}=\operatorname{MLP}^{(K+1)}\left(\sum_{u \in G} \mathbf{h}_{u}^{(K)}\right).$$ (1)
comments.explanation: Equation (1) excludes self-information in the aggregation step. The equation sums over neighbors v ∈ N(u) but processes h_u^(k-1) within each term, which is inconsistent with standard GNN formulations. In typical message passing frameworks, the node update includes either a self-loop (u ∈ N(u)) or a separate self-term. As written, the same h_u^(k-1) would be processed multiple times by MLP^(k) across the sum. Rewrite equation (1) as: $$\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k)} = \operatorname{MLP}^{(k)}_{self}(\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k-1)}) + \sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}(u)} \operatorname{MLP}^{(k)}_{neigh}(\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{v}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{w}_{(v, u)})$$ to properly separate self and neighbor processing.

comments.title: Hypothesis 1 lacks precise mathematical conditions
comments.quote:
Hypothesis 1.

(Linear algorithmic alignment). Let $f:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ be the underlying function and $\mathcal{N}$ a neural network with $m$ MLP modules. Suppose there exist $m$ linear functions $\{g_{i}\}_{i=1}^{m}$ so that by replacing $\mathcal{N}$’s MLP modules with $g_{i}$’s, $\mathcal{N}$ simulates $f$. Given $\epsilon>0$, there exists $\{(x_{i},f(x_{i}))\}_{i=1}^{n}\subset\mathcal{D}\subsetneq\mathcal{X}$ so that $\mathcal{N}$ trained on $\{(x_{i},f(x_{i}))\}_{i=1}^{n}$ by GD with squared loss learns $\hat{f}$ with $\|\hat{f}-f\|<\epsilon$.
comments.explanation: The hypothesis statement lacks mathematical precision in several key aspects. First, it claims existence of a training set without specifying any conditions on the distribution $\mathcal{D}$ or the required size $n$. Second, it claims the network learns $\hat{f}$ with $\|\hat{f}-f\|<\epsilon$ but doesn't specify the norm or domain over which this approximation holds. Third, it doesn't specify any conditions on the training process that are known to affect extrapolation behavior from the earlier theorems. Rewrite Hypothesis 1 to include: (1) $\mathcal{D}$ satisfying the diversity condition of Theorem 2, (2) explicit norm definition (e.g., $\sup_{x\in\mathcal{X}}$), and (3) training in the NTK regime.

comments.title: Definition 3 lacks mathematical rigor
comments.quote:
Definition 3.

Dynamic programming (DP) is a recursive procedure with updates

$\text{Answer}[k][s]=\text{DP-Update}(\{\text{Answer}[k-1][s^{\prime}]\}\,,s^{\prime}=1...n),$ (4)

where $\text{Answer}[k][s]$ is the solution to a sub-problem indexed by iteration $k$ and state $s$, and DP-Update is a task-specific update function that solves the sub-problem based on the previous iteration.
comments.explanation: Definition 3 is mathematically imprecise and incomplete. It defines DP-Update as a "task-specific update function" without specifying any mathematical properties or constraints. The notation $\{\text{Answer}[k-1][s^{\prime}]\}\,,s^{\prime}=1...n$ is ambiguous - it's unclear whether this represents a set, vector, or some other collection of values. Rewrite Definition 3 as: 'Dynamic programming (DP) is a recursive procedure with updates $\text{Answer}[k][s]=\text{DP-Update}(\mathbf{a}_{k-1}, s)$ where $\mathbf{a}_{k-1} = (\text{Answer}[k-1][1], \ldots, \text{Answer}[k-1][n]) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and DP-Update: $\mathbb{R}^n \times \{1,\ldots,n\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a deterministic function that computes the solution to sub-problem $s$ at iteration $k$ based on all sub-problem solutions from iteration $k-1$.'

comments.title: Inconsistent convergence rate claim
comments.quote: First, ReLU MLPs trained by GD converge to linear functions along directions from the origin with a rate of $O(1/t)$.
comments.explanation: The introduction claims a convergence rate of O(1/t), but this rate is not consistent with the paper's own technical results. Theorem 1 provides a convergence rate but the introduction doesn't specify what this rate applies to. The O(1/t) rate appears to be a simplification that may not accurately reflect the actual rate proven in the technical sections. The introduction should either state the exact rate from Theorem 1 or qualify that this is an illustrative rate.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in graph representation sum
comments.quote: $$\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k)}=\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}(u)} \operatorname{MLP}^{(k)}\left(\mathbf{h}_{u}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{v}^{(k-1)}, \mathbf{w}_{(v, u)}\right), \quad \mathbf{h}_{G}=\operatorname{MLP}^{(K+1)}\left(\sum_{u \in G} \mathbf{h}_{u}^{(K)}\right).$$ (1)
comments.explanation: The notation $\sum_{u \in G}$ is mathematically imprecise. Since G = (V,E) is a graph object, not a set, the sum should explicitly iterate over vertices. The earlier notation $\sum_{v \in \mathcal{N}(u)}$ correctly uses set notation. Rewrite the readout term as: $$\mathbf{h}_{G}=\operatorname{MLP}^{(K+1)}\left(\sum_{u \in V} \mathbf{h}_{u}^{(K)}\right)$$ to maintain mathematical precision and notational consistency.

comments.title: Theorem 1 scope gap: direction vector normalization
comments.quote: For any direction $\bm{v}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$, let $\bm{x}_{0}=t\bm{v}$. As $t\rightarrow\infty$, $f(\bm{x}_{0}+h\bm{v})-f(\bm{x}_{0})\rightarrow\beta_{\bm{v}}\cdot h$ for any $h>0$
comments.explanation: The theorem statement doesn't specify whether the direction vector $\bm{v}$ should be normalized. If $\bm{v}$ can have arbitrary magnitude, then $t\bm{v}$ and $h\bm{v}$ scale together, making the interpretation of the convergence rate ambiguous. For example, if we replace $\bm{v}$ with $2\bm{v}$, then $t\bm{v}$ becomes $t(2\bm{v}) = (2t)\bm{v}$, which changes the effective $t$ parameter. Add clarification that $\bm{v}$ is a unit vector (i.e., $\|\bm{v}\| = 1$) or specify how the theorem handles non-unit directions.

comments.title: Lemma 1 orthogonal basis condition needs clarification
comments.quote: Suppose $\{\bm{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$ contains an orthogonal basis $\{\hat{\bm{x}}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{d}$ and $\{-\hat{\bm{x}}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{d}$. If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP $f$ on $\{(\bm{x}_{i},y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with squared loss in the NTK regime, then $f(\bm{x})=\bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{x}$ for all $\bm{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$.
comments.explanation: The orthogonal basis condition in Lemma 1 is insufficiently specified. For the NTK analysis to work and guarantee exact learning, the basis vectors must be linearly independent and non-zero. The current statement allows for degenerate cases like zero vectors or non-normalized vectors that could affect the NTK computation. Add explicit requirement that $\{\hat{\bm{x}}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{d}$ forms a linearly independent set spanning $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $\hat{\bm{x}}_{i} \neq 0$ for all $i$.

comments.title: Convergence notation should be defined
comments.quote: $f(\bm{x})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}\bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{x}$ as $n\rightarrow\infty$.
comments.explanation: The convergence notation $\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}$ is used without definition. While context suggests convergence in probability, this should be explicitly defined when first used. Replace with standard notation $\xrightarrow{p}$ and add 'where $\xrightarrow{p}$ denotes convergence in probability' after the first occurrence.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates the extrapolation behavior of ReLU MLPs and GNNs using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) framework. It establishes that ReLU MLPs tend to linearize outside the training distribution and argues that GNNs succeed in algorithmic extrapolation tasks by aligning their architecture with linear dynamic programming steps.

The paper provides a thoughtful theoretical bridge between training dynamics and extrapolation performance. Its core insight—that successful extrapolation requires architectural alignment with linear operations—is a compelling explanation for the empirical success of GNNs in algorithmic tasks. The work is well-connected to the literature on NTK and algorithmic alignment.

Overview issues:

- Gap between NTK regime and finite-width training: The theoretical results in Theorems 1, 2, and 3 rely on the NTK regime, which assumes infinite width and infinitesimal learning rates. While the authors provide empirical evidence that these behaviors persist in finite-width networks, the paper lacks a formal discussion on the potential breakdown of these properties as networks become narrower or training dynamics move away from the lazy regime. Since the paper claims to explain why GNNs extrapolate in practice, it should address how finite-width effects—such as feature learning and representation evolution—might deviate from the fixed-kernel behavior predicted by the NTK. Adding a brief discussion or a small-scale experiment comparing the NTK prediction to the actual learned kernel evolution would strengthen the theoretical grounding.

- Ambiguity in the definition of 'diverse' training distributions: Theorem 2 requires the training distribution to be 'diverse' enough to cover all directions in the input space. However, the definition of this diversity is somewhat qualitative, relying on the existence of a connected subset that spans the space. It remains unclear how the density or the specific geometry of the training data affects the convergence rate to the linear target. The paper would benefit from a more precise characterization of the required sample complexity or data coverage, perhaps by relating it to the spectral properties of the input covariance matrix.

- Over-reliance on the linear algorithmic alignment hypothesis: Hypothesis 1 posits that successful extrapolation requires the MLP modules to learn nearly linear steps. While this is intuitive for tasks like shortest path, it is not clear if this is a necessary condition for all algorithmic tasks or merely a sufficient one for the specific DP problems tested. The paper should clarify if there are known algorithmic tasks that GNNs solve via extrapolation that do not fit the linear-step paradigm. Without a discussion of potential counterexamples or the limits of this alignment framework, the scope of the hypothesis remains underspecified.

- Limited analysis of non-ReLU activations: Section 3.3 briefly touches on tanh, cosine, and quadratic activations but does not provide a formal analysis comparable to the ReLU case. Since the paper argues that extrapolation is tied to the alignment between activation and target function, the lack of a theoretical framework for non-ReLU activations leaves a significant portion of the design space unexplored. The authors should either provide a more unified view of how different activations influence the 'linearization' behavior outside the training support or explicitly state the limitations of their findings regarding non-ReLU architectures.

- Lack of quantitative sample complexity for the 'diversity' condition: The paper relies on a qualitative definition of 'diverse' training distributions to guarantee extrapolation in Theorem 2. This is a significant gap because the practical utility of the result depends on how much data is needed to cover the space sufficiently. The paper should provide a formal sample complexity bound, perhaps by relating the required number of samples to the condition number of the input covariance matrix or the spectral gap of the data distribution. This would allow readers to understand the feasibility of the 'diverse' condition in high-dimensional settings.

- Missing formal analysis of the 'linear algorithmic alignment' necessity: Hypothesis 1 suggests that linear algorithmic alignment is a key driver for GNN extrapolation, but the paper does not test whether this condition is necessary. It would be valuable to construct a synthetic task where a GNN is known to extrapolate, but the underlying computation is demonstrably non-linear, to see if the model 'linearizes' the task or if it finds a different mechanism. Without addressing whether this alignment is a requirement or just one possible path to success, the scope of the paper's central hypothesis remains ambiguous.

- Absence of a concrete failure case for the linear extrapolation hypothesis: The paper claims that ReLU MLPs converge to linear functions outside the training distribution, which is presented as a barrier to extrapolation. To make this result truly bite, the authors should provide a specific, fully-specified DGP (e.g., a simple non-linear function like a sigmoid or a higher-order polynomial) and analytically compute the predicted linear coefficient $\beta_v$ from Theorem 1. Comparing this analytical prediction to the empirical behavior of a trained MLP would provide a rigorous verification of the 'linearization' claim and demonstrate its impact on extrapolation error.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper presents a strong, coherent argument that aligns theory with empirical observations. A revision should address the gap between the infinite-width NTK assumptions and the practical behavior of finite-width networks, and clarify the scope of the linear algorithmic alignment hypothesis.

Revision targets:
